_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Washington County, PA. Formed 1781 from Westmoreland. County seat at Washington. Most populous western PA county by 1790. National Road corridor. Whiskey Rebellion territory. Excellent record survival.

previous_courthouses.fate: Log courthouse. Washington County was the most populous county in western PA in the 1790 census. Replaced as the county grew.
Stone courthouse. Served during the Whiskey Rebellion and the National Road era. David Bradford, the 'Whiskey Rebel' leader, practiced law here.
Brick courthouse. Served through the Civil War and early industrial era. Replaced by the current Beaux-Arts building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or catastrophic loss
record_losses.description: Washington County's courthouse has not experienced a catastrophic fire or flood. Records run continuously from the county's 1781 formation. The county's early records are among the most complete in western Pennsylvania, reflecting Washington County's status as the most populous and developed county in the region during the late eighteenth century.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Washington County after 1781, the records survive intact. Washington County was the population center of western PA in the 1790s, and its early records are rich with detail — deeds, wills, and court minutes that document a prosperous and politically active frontier society.
record_losses.source: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.

deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Pre-1781 Westmoreland County deeds cover earlier transactions in the Washington County area.
deeds.repository: Washington County Recorder of Deeds; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com PA land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books and estate records from formation
wills_estates.gaps: Early loose papers are less complete than bound volumes. Orphans' Court records supplement estate files.
wills_estates.repository: Washington County Register of Wills; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1885. Pre-1885 marriages documented through church records and newspaper notices.
marriages.gaps: The 104-year gap between county formation (1781) and marriage license law (1885) is filled by church records. Washington County had strong Presbyterian and Associate Reformed congregations with good record-keeping.
marriages.repository: Washington County Clerk of the Orphans' Court; Pennsylvania State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — continuous from formation. Quarter sessions records document Whiskey Rebellion-era proceedings.
court_minutes.repository: Washington County Prothonotary; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

vital_records.repository: Pennsylvania Department of Health; Pennsylvania State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Washington College (later Washington & Jefferson) was established in 1787 — one of the earliest colleges west of the Alleghenies. Student records, alumni records, and institutional archives document the county's educated elite.
substitute_sources.repository: Washington & Jefferson College Archives, Washington, PA
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for tracing the professional and educated class. Matriculation records list students' names, counties of origin, and often fathers' names. Alumni records track careers.

substitute_sources.source_type: National Road records and tavern licenses
substitute_sources.description: The National Road (1818) crossed Washington County, and the county court issued tavern licenses for the road's many inns and stage stops. These licenses name operators, locations, and business partners.
substitute_sources.repository: Washington County Historical Society; Pennsylvania State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Tavern license records place families at specific locations along the National Road and document the transportation economy that defined Washington County in the antebellum era.
substitute_sources.source: Searight, Thomas B. The Old Pike: A History of the National Road. Uniontown, PA, 1894.

substitute_sources.source_type: Federal census and Whiskey Rebellion records
substitute_sources.description: Washington County is enumerated from the 1790 census. Whiskey Rebellion depositions, pardon petitions, and federal court records name hundreds of residents.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Washington County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The 1790 census shows Washington County as the most populous western PA county. Whiskey Rebellion records provide extraordinary detail about the 1794 era.

narrative_hooks: Washington County was the most populous county in western Pennsylvania by the 1790 census — and {ancestor_name}'s deed, will, or court record sits in a courthouse that has never burned, with records running unbroken from 1781.
If {ancestor_name} ran a tavern on the National Road in Washington County, the county court license records document the business — and the National Road turned every crossroads into a small economy.

Pennsylvania's marriage license law did not start until 1885 — if {ancestor_name} married in Washington County before that date, the Presbyterian or Associate Reformed church session records are the primary evidence.

Washington County was the heartland of the Whiskey Rebellion — if {ancestor_name} was in the county in 1794, the federal depositions and pardon petitions may name {pronoun_object} as participant, witness, or victim.
source_references: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.
Slaughter, Thomas P. The Whiskey Rebellion: Frontier Epilogue to the American Revolution. New York: Oxford University Press, 1986.
Searight, Thomas B. The Old Pike: A History of the National Road. Uniontown, PA, 1894.
Crumrine, Boyd. History of Washington County, Pennsylvania. Philadelphia: L.H. Everts & Co., 1882.
